questions.question: What datasets did 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale' use?
questions.gold_answer: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, CIFAR-100
questions.system_answer: The paper reports pre-training on ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M. It also evaluates transfer performance on smaller benchmarks including ImageNet, ImageNet-Real, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.

questions.question: What is the parameter count of the largest variant in 'Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention'?
questions.gold_answer: The largest DeiT variant is DeiT-B★ (DeiT-B with distillation) at 87M parameters.
questions.system_answer: The largest variant listed is DeiT-B★, with 87M parameters. The paper’s reference vision transformer is also reported as 86M parameters, but among the variants shown, 87M is the largest.

questions.question: What architectural innovations does 'Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows' introduce?
questions.gold_answer: hierarchical structure, shifted window attention
questions.system_answer: The paper introduces a hierarchical vision Transformer that computes self-attention in non-overlapping local windows, then alternates with shifted window partitioning to create cross-window connections. It also uses patch merging to build multi-scale feature maps, and this design yields linear computational complexity with respect to image size. Relative position bias is another architectural choice highlighted as beneficial.

questions.question: Briefly describe the architecture of 'Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers'.
questions.gold_answer: DINO is a self-supervised vision transformer training method
questions.system_answer: The paper presents DINO, a simple self-supervised architecture for Vision Transformers based on self-distillation without labels: a student network learns to match a momentum-teacher network across multiple augmented views, with centering and sharpening to prevent collapse. It is designed to work with both ViTs and convnets, but the paper emphasizes that ViTs with small patches benefit especially strongly.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on top-1 accuracy on ImageNet?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy in this corpus varies widely from 1.3% to 91.0% across 594 results.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows a very wide spread in reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy, from 1.3 up to 91.0 (stddev 8.69), so the papers do not agree on a single value. There are also conflicting SOTA claims: CoCa (finetuned) is listed at 91.0, while other papers report lower top claims such as PaLI-17B at 90.9, CoCa (frozen) at 90.6, and MaxViT-XL at 88.7/88.51.

questions.question: What is the range of top-1 accuracy values reported on CIFAR-100 in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: The top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-100 in this corpus ranges from 48.5% to 91.88% across 27 results.
questions.system_answer: Across the corpus, reported CIFAR-100 top-1 accuracy values range from 48.5% to 91.88%, a spread of 43.38 percentage points. The highest value in the evidence is 91.88% (AdaptFormer-Swin), and no explicit SOTA claims are listed in the provided evidence.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on which model holds SOTA on ADE20K mIoU?
questions.gold_answer: No—this corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model, since 7 papers each claim SOTA on the benchmark.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows conflicting SOTA claims for ADE20K mIoU: InternImage-H is listed at 62.9, while ViT-Adapter-L is also repeatedly claimed as SOTA at 61.5. Other strong results (e.g., SwinV2-G at 59.9) are lower, so the disagreement is specifically about which top model is current best and what the exact best value is.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on mIoU values on ADE20K?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across 97 results, from 26.6 to 62.9.
questions.system_answer: No. The ADE20K mIoU values in the corpus are not consistent: they range from 26.6 to 62.9, a spread of 36.3 points (stddev 7.29). The highest reported SOTA is InternImage-H at 62.9, while other papers claim lower SOTA values such as ViT-Adapter-L at 61.5 and SwinV2-G at 59.9, so the corpus shows substantial variation and multiple competing SOTA claims.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on whether position embeddings are necessary for vision transformers?
questions.gold_answer: Papers disagree on the necessity of position embeddings; some ablations show minimal impact while others demonstrate clear performance drops without them.
questions.system_answer: No, the corpus does not fully agree. Some papers treat position embeddings as part of the standard ViT design or as necessary to add positional information, e.g. ViT says it “adds position embeddings,” and CPVT notes that “positional encodings are commonly employed” because self-attention is permutation-equivariant. But other papers argue they are not necessary or can be removed with little/no loss: CvT says “Removing position embedding of CvT does not degrade performance,” and the self-supervised ViT study says removing them “only slightly hurts performance.”

questions.question: How did the maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet change year over year in this corpus?
questions.system_answer: The maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet rose from 88.08 in 2020 to 90.0 in 2021 and 91.0 in 2022, then declined to 87.8 in 2023 and 83.9 in 2024. Overall, the trend is up through 2022 and then down in the last two years.

questions.question: What is the trend in average model parameter count by year in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: Average model parameter count increased substantially from 2020 to 2023, rising from ~161M in 2020 to ~4099M in 2023, before dropping in 2024 due to small sample size.
questions.system_answer: The average model parameter count rises sharply from 161.1M in 2020 to 291.0M in 2021, then to 780.6M in 2022, and jumps again to 4,098.8M in 2023. It then drops steeply to 50.0M in 2024, so the overall trend is upward through 2023 followed by a sharp decline in 2024.
